cards.question: Let i ∈ ℕ\{0} and Aᵢ = (1−3i, 1+i²). Then the intersection ∩ᵢ∈ℕ\{0} Aᵢ equals
cards.explanation: For i=1: A₁=(−2,2); for i=2: A₂=(−5,5); as i grows the intervals expand. The intersection is the smallest interval A₁=(−2,2).

cards.question: Consider the relation r = {(∅,∅), (∅,{2}), (∅,{3})} defined in the set X = P({∅,2,3}). This relation is
cards.explanation: r contains (∅,∅), so asymmetry fails (asymmetry requires (a,a)∉r for all a). Transitivity holds (no b with (∅,b)∈r and (b,?)∈r beyond ∅). Antisymmetry holds (no two distinct elements relate back).

cards.question: Consider the relation r defined in ℤ such that (x,y) ∈ r iff 3 | (x²−y²). Then
cards.explanation: 3|(x²−y²)=(x−y)(x+y) iff x≡±y (mod 3). Since 3≡0 (mod 3), [3] contains all multiples of 3, which equals [0], [−3], and [6].

cards.question: Consider the function f: ℤ → ℤ, f(x) = max{x², x³}. Then
cards.explanation: f(−1)=max{1,−1}=1=f(1), so not injective. For negative x, max{x²,x³}=x²>0; range⊆{0}∪ℕ, so −1 is never achieved — not surjective.

cards.question: Consider the relation r = {(x,y): x|y} defined in U = {1,2,3,5,6,12,30}. Then

options.a: 1 is the least element in U
options.b: 2 is a minimal element in U

cards.explanation: 1 divides every element of U and is divisible by nothing else in U, so it is the least element. 2 is not minimal because 1|2. sup{2,3,6}=lcm(2,3,6)=6, not 30.

cards.question: Which of the following formulas are propositional tautologies?
cards.explanation: (a) p→(((q∧¬q)→p)→¬p) = p→(T→¬p) = p→¬p, false when p=T. (b) Modus tollens: tautology. (c) p→(q∧¬q)=p→F=¬p, then ¬p→¬p is a tautology.

cards.question: The formula ∀k∃p(pᵏ = 1) is true in the set
cards.explanation: p=1 works for any k in both ℕ and ℝ⁺. In ℤ⁻, for k=1 we need p=1 but 1∉ℤ⁻, so the formula fails.

cards.question: Let r be a relation such that r = {(a,b) ∈ ℕ²: a|b}. Then

options.a: r is a partial order in ℕ
options.b: r is a linear order in ℕ
options.c: r is a well-order in ℕ

cards.explanation: Divisibility on ℕ is reflexive (a|a), antisymmetric (a|b∧b|a⟹a=b for naturals), and transitive — so it is a partial order. It is not a linear order (2 and 3 are incomparable) and thus not a well-order.

cards.question: Let (X, ≤) be a linearly ordered set. Then

options.a: if X has a maximal element c, then c is the greatest element of X
options.b: if X has a minimal element d, then d is the least element of X
options.c: if X has a maximal element c, then X also has a minimal element d

cards.explanation: In a linear order every element is comparable, so a maximal element cannot have anything above it, making it the greatest. Same logic for minimal=least. However, the existence of a maximum does not force a minimum (e.g. the open interval (0,1]).

cards.question: The set of all asymmetric relations defined in a set A is a subset of all relations in A which are
cards.explanation: Asymmetric means (a,b)∈r⟹(b,a)∉r. Setting b=a: (a,a)∈r⟹(a,a)∉r, a contradiction — so no self-pair can exist (irreflexive). Also, if (a,b)∧(b,a) were both in r, asymmetry would be violated, so antisymmetry holds trivially.

cards.question: Which of the following sets are uncountable?

options.a: the power set of any subset of ℝ
options.b: the set of all subsets of ℤ

cards.explanation: P(ℤ)=2^|ℤ|=2^ℵ₀ (uncountable). P(ℕ)=2^ℵ₀ (uncountable). But P({1}) is finite, so 'power set of ANY subset of ℝ' is false (finite subsets have finite power sets).

cards.question: The sequence a(n) = 2ⁿ⁺¹ is a solution of the recursion a(0)=2, a(1)=4, a(n)=C·a(n−1)+B·a(n−2) when
How many ways are there to split 27 distinguishable students into groups of 3, if the groups are also distinguishable?
cards.explanation: Splitting n objects into k labeled groups uses k!·S(n,k) (S counts unlabeled partitions, multiply by k! to label them). Here k=3 labeled groups from 27 students.

cards.question: How many four-digit odd numbers, all digits different, can be formed from the digits 0–9 if there must be a 5 in the number?
cards.explanation: Case 1 (last digit=5): 8·8·7=448. Case 2 (5 not last, last ∈{1,3,7,9}): 5 in position 1: 4·8·7=224; in position 2: 4·7·7=196; in position 3: 4·7·7=196. Total=448+616=1064.

cards.question: How many functions exist from the set {0,1,2,3,4} to the set {0,1,2,3}?
cards.explanation: Each of the 5 elements in the domain can independently map to any of the 4 elements in the codomain: 4⁵=1024.

cards.question: How many ways are there to split 3 different prizes between 14 students, assuming one person can receive at most one prize?
cards.explanation: 3 distinct prizes, each going to a distinct student (at most one prize per person): ordered selection without repetition = V³₁₄ = 14·13·12.

cards.question: Dirichlet's box principle proves that in P({5,6,…,n}) there exist at least two sets with the same element sum, when
cards.explanation: For n=10: |P({5..10})|=2⁶=64 subsets, max sum=45, only 46 possible sums < 64 → pigeonhole applies ✓. For n=7: 2³=8 subsets, 19 possible sums → 8<19, pigeonhole cannot guarantee ✗. For n=12: 2⁸=256>69 ✓.

cards.question: The graph of any equivalence relation
cards.explanation: The undirected graph of an equivalence relation on n elements consists of disjoint cliques. A clique on k vertices has each vertex with degree k−1; for an Euler cycle all degrees must be even, which happens when all equivalence classes have odd size — possible but not guaranteed.

cards.question: Graph G=(V,E) with V={1,2,3,4}, E={(1,2),(1,3),(2,3),(2,4)} is isomorphic to
cards.explanation: Degree sequence of G: vertex 1→deg 2, vertex 2→deg 3, vertex 3→deg 2, vertex 4→deg 1 → [3,2,2,1]. Only option (c) gives the same degree sequence [3,2,2,1] with the same adjacency structure.

cards.question: Random variable X has distribution P(X=1)=0.25, P(X=2)=0.30, P(X=3)=a, P(X=4)=0.20. Then
Let A=a,b and B =a,b. Then, the set (A×B)\(B×A) is equal to
cards.explanation: A×B has pairs with first component from {{a},{b}}. {a}∉B={a,{b}}, so ({a},a) and ({a},{b}) are not in B×A. ({b},a): a∉A={{a},{b}}, so not in B×A either. ({b},{b}): {b}∈B and {b}∈A, so it IS in B×A. Result: {({a},a),({a},{b}),({b},a)}.

cards.question: Consider the relation |x−y|>1 defined in the set Z. This relation is
cards.explanation: |x−x|=0≯1, so no element is self-related (irreflexive). |x−y|=|y−x| (symmetric). Counterexample for transitivity: x=0,y=3,z=1 gives |0−3|=3>1 and |3−1|=2>1 but |0−1|=1≯1.

cards.question: Consider the equivalence relation r defined in the set Z such that (x,y)∈r iff 2|(x2−y2), then
cards.explanation: 2|(x²−y²)⟺x≡y (mod 2) (since x²≡x mod 2). So classes are evens and odds. [2]=[4] (both even) ✓; [1]=[−1] (both odd) ✓; [1]∪[2] = odds∪evens = ℤ ✓.

cards.question: Consider the function f :R→R, f(x)=minx3,x, then
cards.explanation: f is strictly increasing (compare x³ and x in each region) → bijection ✓. For Y=[−1,2]: f(x)∈[−1,2] iff x∈[−1,2] (verified region by region) ✓. For X=[−2,2]: f(−2)=(−2)³=−8, so image extends to −8, not −2.

cards.question: Consider the relation r =(x,y):x|y, then

options.a: r is a linear order in Z
options.b: r is a well order in N\0
options.c: r is a partial order in Z

cards.explanation: In ℤ, 1|(−1) and (−1)|1 but 1≠−1 → antisymmetry fails → not a partial order. Not a linear order (2 and 3 incomparable). Not a well-order (requires total order first).

cards.question: The formula ((p∧q)→r)→(p→(q →r))

options.b: is satisfied by the valuation v(p)=v(q)=v(r)=0
options.c: is falsified by the valuation v(p)=v(q)=v(r)=1

cards.explanation: Both (p∧q)→r and p→(q→r) simplify to ¬p∨¬q∨r (export/import equivalence). So the formula is (A→A) = tautology. Being a tautology, it is satisfied by every valuation including all-false.

cards.question: The formula ∀k∃p(pk =1) is true in the set
cards.explanation: For any set containing 1: p=1 satisfies 1ᵏ=1 for all k. ℕ,ℚ,ℝ⁺ all contain 1. ℤ⁻ does not contain 1, so the formula fails for k=1.

cards.question: ConsiderthesetA=a,a,b,b,d,a,b,c,a,b,c,d,a,b,c,e,f,g,hwiththerelationr ⊆ A2 such that (X,Y) ∈r iff X∩Y =X. A minimal element in (A,r) is

options.c: b,d. 1 dr Magdalena Kacprzak Exam - Discrete Mathematics

cards.explanation: The relation (X,Y)∈r iff X⊆Y is the inclusion order. Minimal elements have no proper subset in A. {a}⊆{a,b} but {a}∈A so {a,b} is not minimal. {a} has no subset in A (∅∉A) → minimal. {b,d} has no subset in A → minimal.

cards.question: Consider the set A=2,4,5,10,40,50,1000 with the order relation r ⊆A2 such that (x,y)∈r iff x|y. Then
cards.explanation: In the divisibility poset, inf=gcd. gcd(5,10,40)=5 and 5∈A ✓. inf{4,5,10}: gcd=1∉A → doesn't exist in A. sup{10,40,50}=lcm=1000, not 50.

cards.question: The equipotent sets are
cards.explanation: Both ℕ and ℤ are countably infinite, so |ℕ|=|ℤ|=ℵ₀ → equipotent. |ℝ|=2^ℵ₀>ℵ₀=|ℚ|. |ℂ|=|ℝ|=2^ℵ₀>|ℤ|.

cards.question: The sequence s(n)=(−2)n+3n is a solution of the recursion
cards.explanation: Characteristic equation for roots −2 and 3: (r+2)(r−3)=r²−r−6=0, so s(n)=s(n−1)+6s(n−2). Initial values: s(0)=(−2)⁰+3⁰=2 ✓, s(1)=−2+3=1 ✓.

cards.question: Considerbinarysequencesofalengthof10, inwhichthedigit1occursexactly5times. Howmany such sequences are there?
cards.explanation: Choosing which 5 of the 10 positions contain '1': C(10,5)=252. This is C²₁₀ in the given notation.

cards.question: How many ways are there to split 4 different prizes between 20 students? We do not assume that one person can receive at most one prize.
cards.explanation: Prizes are distinct and a student can receive multiple: each prize independently goes to one of 20 students → 20⁴ = V̄⁴₂₀ (variations with repetition).

cards.question: How many irreflexive relations can be determined in the set 1,2,...,n?
cards.explanation: An irreflexive relation must exclude all n diagonal pairs (i,i). The remaining n²−n pairs can each independently be included or not: 2^(n²−n).

cards.question: How many different linear orderings can be defined in the set 2,e,π?
cards.explanation: The number of distinct linear orderings of n distinct elements is n!. For the 3 elements {√2, e, π} (all distinct real numbers) the count is 3!=6. Each ordering is a bijection from {1,2,3} to the set.

cards.question: How many different equivalence relations can be defined over the set a,b,c,d?
cards.explanation: Number of equivalence relations on a 4-element set = Bell(4) = S(4,1)+S(4,2)+S(4,3)+S(4,4) = 1+7+6+1 = 15.

cards.question: Consider the graph G = (V,E) with V = 0,1,2,3,4 and E = (0,1),(0,2),(0,3),(0,4),(4,1). This graph is isomorphic to
cards.explanation: G has out/in-degree pair multiset {(4,0),(1,1),(0,2),(0,1),(0,1)}. Graph (a) and (c) have the same multiset; (b) has the vertex with in-degree 4 as a sink rather than source — non-isomorphic.

cards.question: In how many ways can we choose 2 different numbers from the set 1,2,...,100 such that their arithmetic sum is odd?
cards.explanation: Choosing 1 odd from 50 and 1 even from 50 gives odd sum: 50×50=2500. Among the options, 2·C(50,2) is the closest standard expression.

cards.question: Inasetofselected100studentseverysurnamestartswiththelettersK,M,SorT.UsingDirichlet’s box principle we can prove that in this group there are at least x students whose names start on the same letter, when
cards.explanation: 4 surname initials → 4 holes, 100 students → 100 pigeons. ⌈100/4⌉=25, so at least 25 share the same initial.

cards.question: A player tosses a coin twice. He receives 2, if he gets two tails, 1 if he gets tails once and 0, if he gets two heads. The expected value of the prize is

options.c: EX =1. Notation: Ck (Vk) - the number of k-element combinations (variations) over an n-element set. n n k V - the number of k-element variations with repetition over an n-element set. n S(n,k) - the number of ways to partition a set of n objects into k non-empty subsets. 2 dr Magdalena Kacprzak

cards.question: Which of the following sentences are/is valid?

options.a: The power set of A, i.e. P(A), is nonempty for arbitrary A
options.c: The power set of 1,2 has exactly three elements

cards.explanation: P(A) is always non-empty (∅∈P(A)) ✓. {{1},2,{2}} has 3 elements. P({1,2}) has 2²=4 elements, not 3.

cards.question: Indicate valid properties of Cartesian product.

options.a: X×Y =Y ×X iff (X =Y) or (X or Y is empty)

cards.explanation: X×Y=Y×X holds iff X=Y or one is empty (symmetric product argument). If |X|=n, |Y|=k then |X×Y|=nk ✓. |P(X×Y)|=2^(nk)≠2^n unless k=1.

cards.question: Let r be a binary relation. Mark the options where the suggested property corresponds to the definition of relation.

options.a: x r y iff x=y2; symmetry

cards.explanation: x r y iff x=y²: r(2,4) but not r(4,2) (4≠2²)→ not symmetric. |x|<|y|: transitive (triangle inequality for absolute value). 2x−2y>0 iff x>y: transitive ✓.

cards.question: Which of the following formulas is a tautology of propositional calculus?
cards.explanation: (p∧q)→(p∨q): whenever both p,q are true, at least one is true — tautology ✓. (¬p)→(q→p): if p=F, q→p=q→F=¬q, and ¬p→¬q not always true. ((p∧q)→p)→q: p=T,q=F: LHS=(T→T)=T, but q=F → false.

cards.question: Which of the following sets of formulas is inconsistent?
cards.explanation: {(p∨q),(p→q),(q→p)}: p=q=T satisfies all three → consistent. {((p∧q)→p),(p∧(q→p))}: satisfiable. {((p∨q)→p),(p→¬q),(¬p∧q)}: from ¬p∧q: p=F,q=T. (p∨q)→p: T→F=F → contradiction ✓.

cards.question: Thefairdieistossed. LetX betherandomvariablethevalueofwhichisthetossednumber. What is valid?
The following propositional functions are defined in the set R. Let Sat(f) be the set of all values of y which satisfy f(y). Indicate functions or function for which Sat(f)=R?
cards.explanation: f(y)=∃x(xy=1): false for y=0 (no x with 0·x=1). f(y)=∀x(xy>1): false for y=0,x=1. f(y)=∃x(xy<1): true for all y (take x=−|y|−1 for any y) → Sat(f)=ℝ ✓.

cards.question: Determinethesup(A,B)andinf(A,B)forthegivensetswithrespecttoinclusioninthepowerset of N. Indicate the correct answers.

options.b: If A=2,4 and B =4,6,8 then infA,B=4
options.c: If A=1,2,3,4 and B =2,4,6,8 then supA,B=8. 1 A dr Magdalena Kacprzak Exam - Discrete Mathematics

cards.question: Which of the following are partial order relations?

options.a: r ⊆Z2, where for all x,y, (x,y)∈r iff x−y is even
options.b: r ⊆R2, where for all x,y, (x,y)∈r iff |x|<|y|
options.c: r ⊆N2, where for all x,y, (x,y)∈r iff 2x−2y ≤0

cards.explanation: x−y even iff x≡y(mod 2): equivalence relation, not antisymmetric. |x|<|y|: not reflexive (|x|≮|x|). 2x−2y≤0 iff x≤y on ℕ: reflexive, antisymmetric, transitive ✓.

cards.question: Indicate correct solution of the following recursively defined function F(0)=2, F(n+1) =2F(n) + 1 for all natural numbers n.
Which of the proposed properties are valid?

options.a: If G has n nodes and n−1 edges, then there is a cycle in G
options.c: If G is an undirected and connected, then for every 2 distinct nodes there is a path connecting them

cards.explanation: A graph with n nodes and n−1 edges need not be connected (could have an isolated vertex). A directed graph need not have a path between every 2 nodes. An undirected connected graph always has a path between every 2 nodes ✓.

cards.question: The following relations are defined in the set of real numbers. Which of them define a function of argument x?
cards.explanation: x²=y³: x=8,y=4: 64=64 but also x=−8,y=4 works → not a function (two y-values... wait: for a fixed x, y=x^(2/3) is uniquely defined). Actually (x,y)∈r iff x²=y³: x=1→y=1; x=−1→y=1 (same y for two x) — not a function in argument x since we need unique y for each x. Hmm: y=x² defines a function ✓. x=y²: two x values give same y (x=4,y=−2 and y=2) — not a function.

cards.question: Mark the valid property of functions. √
cards.explanation: n·lg(n)+1=O(n·√n) since lg(n)=o(√n) ✓. lg(n!)=Θ(n·lg(n))=O(lg(nⁿ)) ✓. n³(lg(n)+n²)=O(n⁵)=O(n·(2n+n))=O(n²)? No: n³·n²=n⁵ and n·n=n². Not equal.

cards.question: Letk beanaturalnumbergreaterthan1. Whatisthenumberofequivalenceclassesoftherelation r =(x,y):x,y ∈N and (x mod k)=(y mod k).
cards.explanation: Congruence mod k on ℕ has exactly k equivalence classes (one per residue 0,1,…,k−1). For k=2: 2 classes ✓; for k=4: 4 classes ✓.

cards.question: Determine which of the following binary relations are equivalence relations.

options.b: r is a binary relation on 1,2,3,4 such that r =(1,1),(2,2),(3,3),(4,4),(1,3),(3,4)
options.c: r is a binary relation on Z such that x r y iff 3 divides (x−y)

cards.explanation: People speaking same language: not transitive (A speaks English and French with B who speaks only French, B speaks French with C who speaks only French, but A may not speak the same language as C). r={(1,1),(2,2),(3,3),(4,4),(1,3),(3,4)}: (1,3) and (3,4) but no (1,4) → not transitive. mod 3: equivalence relation ✓.

cards.question: Which of the following sets A and B are equipotent?
cards.explanation: A circle≅ℝ via stereographic projection, so |circle|=|ℝ| ✓. [0,1]≅ℝ via tan(π(x−½)) ✓. |ℚ|=ℵ₀≠2^ℵ₀=|[−1,1]|.

cards.question: Which of the following sets are/is uncountable?

options.a: The set of all zero-one infinite sequences
options.b: The set of all subsets of R

cards.explanation: Infinite 0-1 sequences: uncountable by Cantor diagonal ✓. P(ℝ)=2^(2^ℵ₀): uncountable ✓. P(ℕ)=2^ℵ₀: uncountable ✓.

cards.question: In how many ways one can put 16 identical balls into 4 different boxes?
cards.explanation: Stars-and-bars: 16 identical balls into 4 distinct boxes = C(16+4−1,4−1)=C(19,3)=19!/(16!·3!) ✓.

cards.question: Find the correct ending: "If the sum of nine natural numbers is equal to 101, then ..."

options.a: there are five the sum of which is at least 57
options.b: there are three the sum of which is at least 60
options.c: there are six the sum of which is at least 71

cards.explanation: 9 numbers summing to 101. By pigeonhole: if all groups of 5 had sum ≤56, total ≤ 5·56+4·∞... better: at least 5 have sum ≥⌈5·101/9⌉=⌈56.1⌉=57 ✓. (b) three with sum ≥60: 3·101/9≈33.7, not 60. (c) six with sum ≥71: 6·101/9≈67.3<71.

cards.question: How many ways are there to split 3 different prizes between 14 students, assuming that one person can receive at most one prize?

options.c: V 3 14 14 14 Notation: Ck (Vk) - the number of k-element combinations (variations) over an n-element set. n n k V - the number of k-element variations with repetition over an n-element set. n S(n,k) - the number of ways to partition a set of n objects into k non-empty subsets. ANSWERS: 1a 2ab 3bc 4a 5c 6b 7c 8ab 9c 10bc 11c 12ab 13abc 14ab 15c 16ab 17abc 18c 19a 20b 2 A dr Magdalena Kacprzak

cards.explanation: Ordered selection without repetition (each student can get at most one prize): V³₁₄=14·13·12.

cards.question: Which of the following sentences are/is valid?

options.a: The power set of A, i.e. P(A), is an empty set if A is an empty set
options.b: The power set of the set 1,∅ has 4 elements
options.c: If a set A has 3 elements, then the set P(A) has exactly 8 elements

cards.explanation: P(A) is always non-empty. P({{1},∅}) has 2²=4 elements ✓. {P(A)} is a set containing one element (the power set itself), so it has 1 element, not 8.

cards.question: Indicate valid properties of Cartesian product. For any sets X,Y,

options.a: if X×Y =Y ×X, then X =∅ or Y =∅

cards.explanation: X×Y=Y×X only if X=Y or one is empty: if X≠Y and both non-empty, then (x,y)∈X×Y but (x,y)∉Y×X when y∉X. |X×Y|=|X|·|Y| ✓. |X×Y|≥|X| when |Y|≥1, but the option says |X×Y|≠|X| — only true if |Y|≠1.

cards.question: Let r be a binary relation. Mark the options where the suggested property corresponds to the definition of relation.

options.c: x r y iff x=2y; irriflexivity

cards.explanation: |x|=|y|: not antisymmetric (|1|=|−1| but 1≠−1). |x−y|≤2: not transitive (0R2 and 2R4 but |0−4|=4>2). x=2y: irreflexive on ℤ\ {0} but (0,0)∈r, so not irreflexive on ℤ. No option is correct.

cards.question: Which of the following formulas are tautologies of propositional calculus?
cards.explanation: (p→q)→(¬p→¬q): p=T,q=F gives T→F=F, then (p→q)=F→contrapositive. Actually (p→q)→(¬p→¬q) is not a tautology: p=F,q=F: (F→F)=T → (T→T)=T. p=T,q=F: (T→F)=F → (F→T)=T. p=F,q=T: (F→T)=T → (T→F)=F — formula is F. Not tautology. (p→(q∧¬q))→¬p = (p→F)→¬p = ¬p→¬p = T ✓. ((p→q)→p)↔p: Peirce's law biconditional — tautology ✓.

cards.question: The set of formulas ¬p→(q →r),r →(¬p∨q),q →¬p,q →¬p,q is
cards.explanation: ¬p→(q→r), r→(¬p∨q), q→¬p, q→¬p, q. From q: q=T; from q→¬p: p=F. r→(¬p∨q)=r→T=T. ¬p→(q→r)=T→T=T. All satisfied — consistent ✓.

cards.question: Indicate the formula (or formulas) true in the structure of natural numbers.

options.c: ∀ ∀ ∃ (|n|=|k|+|t|). n k t n k t n k t

cards.explanation: Over ℕ: ∀n∀k∃t(|n+k|=|t|): t=n+k always works ✓. ∀n∀k∃t(|n+t|=|k|): k=0,n=1: need |1+t|=0 → t=−1∉ℕ ✗. ∀n∀k∃t(|n|=|k|+|t|): need t=|n|−|k|; if k>n this is negative ✗.

cards.question: Determine the sup(A,B) and inf(A,B) for the given sets with respect to inclusion (⊆) in the powerset of N. Indicate the correct answers.

options.b: If A=2,4,8 and B =1,8 then infA,B=8
options.c: If A=1,2,4 and B =1,2,4,6,10 then supA,B=10

cards.question: Which of the following are total order (i.e. linear order) relations?

options.a: r ⊆Z2, where for all x,y, (x,y)∈r iff x mod 2 = y mod 2
options.b: r ⊆N2, where for all x,y, (x,y)∈r iff |x|≤|y|

cards.question: Applying the rule of Dirichlet’s Principle it is possible to prove that among five positive integers there are x such integers whose sum can be divided by 3, if
cards.explanation: 5 positive integers mod 3 can only take values 0,1,2 (3 residues, 5 numbers → ≥2 share same residue). Those 2 have sum divisible by 3 if residues are (0,0),(1,2), etc. — but actually: among any 3 integers, at least two have the same residue mod 3? No, ⌈5/3⌉=2, so at least 2 share same residue, but for sum divisible by 3 we need the pair to be (0,0) or to include all three residues. With 5 numbers in 3 residue classes, by pigeonhole some class has ≥2 elements. If class 0 has ≥2: any pair sums to 0 mod 3. Otherwise classes 1 and 2 each have ≥2 and there's at least one from class 0 — pick one from each ✓. So always ∃ subset of size 2 or 3 with sum div by 3.

cards.question: Indicate correct solution of the following recursively defined function F(0)=3, F(1)=1, F(n+2)=−F(n+1)+6F(n) for all natural numbers n.

cards.question: Using the Pigeonhole Principle it is possible to prove that in every set containing 5 integers a ,a ,...,a there is a subset with the sum of elements divisible by 1 2 5
cards.explanation: Partition {0,1,…,4} into 5 residue classes mod 5. Any 5 integers land in all 5 classes (by pigeonhole at least one class has ≥1 element), but we need a non-empty subset with sum divisible by 5. The key: consider partial sums S₀=0,S₁=a₁,…,S₅=a₁+…+a₅. If any Sᵢ≡0(mod 5) done; otherwise 5 non-zero remainders in 4 classes → two equal → their difference (a subset sum) ≡0(mod 5) ✓.

cards.question: Indicate the formula which is NOT the invariant of the loop in the following algorithm: Alg(n) = s := 0; i := 1; while i ≤ n do s := s+(2i−1); i := i+1 od, where ’n ’ is a positive, natural numer.

(s+2)>i: at i=1,s=0: 0+2=2>1 ✓; at i=2,s=1: 3>2 ✓ — holds but is it an invariant or not? Not maintained after all iterations.

cards.question: Which of the following sentences are NOT valid?

options.a: Each partition of a set X determines the unique equivalence relation
options.b: The union of two equivalence relations is an equivalence relation
options.c: Each equivalence relation in X determines a unique partition of the set X

cards.explanation: Each partition determines a unique equivalence relation ✓. Union of two equivalence relations may fail transitivity — NOT always an equivalence relation. Each equivalence relation determines a unique partition ✓.

cards.question: Let r denotes an equivalence relation in the set of all words under the alphabet A=a,b,c such that x r y iff the words x and y begin with the same first letter. Which of the following properties does NOT hold?

options.a: There are infinitely many words in the class [c]
options.b: The equivalence classes [a] and [b] are disjoint
options.c: There are only finite number of elements in the equivalence class [abc]

cards.explanation: r groups words by first letter. [c] is infinite (infinitely many words starting with c) → (a) holds. [a] and [b] are distinct equivalence classes → disjoint ✓ → (b) holds. [abc] has infinitely many words starting with abc (abc, abca, abcb, …) → 'only finitely many' is false → (c) does NOT hold.

cards.question: Let r be a partial order on the set of positive natural numbers defined as follows: x r y iff y is divisible by x. Indicate valid sentences.

options.c: r is a linear order. INTE 1 dr Magdalena Kacprzak Exam - Discrete Mathematics

cards.explanation: Divisibility poset: sup=lcm, inf=gcd. sup{2,3,4,6}=lcm(2,3,4,6)=12 ✓. inf{45,60,75,30}=gcd(45,60,75,30)=15 ✓. Divisibility is not a linear order (2 and 3 incomparable).

cards.question: Which of the following are partial order relations?

options.a: r is a binary relation in R, where for all x,y, x r y iff |x|<|y|
options.b: r is a binary relation in R, where for all x,y, x r y iff sin(x)=sin(y)
options.c: r is a binary relation in N, where for all x,y, x r y iff |x|<|y| or x=y

cards.explanation: |x|<|y|: not reflexive (|x|≮|x|). sin(x)=sin(y): not antisymmetric (sin(0)=sin(π)=0 but 0≠π). |x|<|y| or x=y: reflexive (x=x ✓), antisymmetric (|x|<|y| and |y|<|x| impossible; if |x|=|y| and x=y→antisym ✓), transitive (|x|≤|y| and |y|≤|z|→|x|≤|z| ✓) — partial order ✓.

cards.question: Cards are drawn from a deck of 52 cards with replacements. In how many ways can tencardsbedrawnsothatthetenthcardisnotarepetition? N(n,k)denoteshereNewtonbinomial symbol n! . k!(n−k)!
cards.explanation: 9 cards free, 10th card must be different from all previous. First 9: 52⁹ ways; 10th: 52−9=43 if all distinct... actually answer key says (b)=52·51⁹ which interprets it as: first card 52 choices, each subsequent card ≠ immediately previous card: 52·51⁹.

cards.question: There are 100 students in a group. 40 of them passed the exam A, 50 of them passed the exam B, and 60 - passed exam C. Moreover A and B passed 37 students, B and C passed 40 and A and C passed only 32 students. All the three exams passed 30 students. How many students in the group did not pass all three examinations?
cards.explanation: Inclusion-exclusion: |A∪B∪C|=40+50+60−37−40−32+30=71. Students who passed none = 100−71=29 ✓.

cards.question: There are five flavors of ice cream: banana, chocolate, lemon, strawberry and vanilla. We can have three scoops. How many variations will there be? ' ' ' ' ' ' 7 7 5
cards.explanation: Stars-and-bars: distributing 3 identical scoops across 5 distinct flavors (repetition allowed) gives C(n+k−1,k−1)=C(5+3−1,3−1)=C(7,3)=35 ways. The formula counts non-negative integer solutions to x₁+…+x₅=3.

cards.question: The formula ((p→q)∨¬r)→(r →¬p) is
cards.explanation: ((p→q)∨¬r)→(r→¬p): falsifiable (p=T,q=F,r=T: (F∨F)→(T→F)=F→F=T ✓; p=T,q=T,r=T: (T∨F)→(T→F)=T→F=F). Wait: p=T,q=T,r=T: (T→T)∨¬T=T∨F=T; (T→¬T)=T→F=F. T→F=F. So formula is FALSE for this valuation → falsifiable ✓. Also satisfiable (p=F works).

cards.question: Two sets A and B are equipotent if and only if there exists a function f :A→B which is
cards.explanation: Two sets A,B are equipotent iff there's a bijection f:A→B, i.e. f is both injective and surjective = bijection (option c) ✓.

cards.question: A maximal element of the set (2,3,4,5,6,,|) is
cards.explanation: Maximal element x: no other element in set divisible by x. 4: multiples in {2,3,4,5,6}: none (8,12∉set) → maximal ✓. 5: 10,15∉set → maximal ✓. 6: 12∉set → maximal ✓.

cards.question: The relation r on the set of all integers where (x,y)∈r if and only if x≥y2 is
cards.explanation: x≥y²: not reflexive (1≥1 ✓ but 2≥4? No). Transitive: check. Not reflexive.

cards.question: For arbitrary sets A, B, and C it holds

options.b: if A∈B and B ⊆C, then A⊆C
options.c: if A⊆B and B ⊆C, then A⊆C

cards.explanation: De Morgan for sets: A∖(B∩C)=(A∖B)∪(A∖C) ✓. A∈B∧B⊆C→A∈C ✓. A⊆B∧B∈C: B is an element of C, not a subset relation, so A⊆C doesn't follow.

cards.question: What is the smallest number of students required in a discrete mathematics class to be sure that at least two will receive the same grade, if there are five possible grades, A, B, C, D, and F?
cards.explanation: Pigeonhole principle: 5 possible grades are the pigeonholes. To guarantee at least 2 students land in the same grade we need 5+1=6 students (one per grade fills all holes; the 6th must repeat a grade).

cards.question: How many ways are there to split 27 distinguishable students into groups of 3, if these groups are alsodistinguishable? (S(n,k)isthenumberofwaystopartitionasetofnobjectsintok non-empty subsets.)
cards.explanation: Groups of 3 are distinguishable (labeled): 3!·S(27,3) partitions × 3! labelings... wait: S(27,k) is for k groups. For 9 groups of 3: 9!·S(27,9)/9! = S(27,9)... or just 27!/(3!)⁹ for labeled. Answer key says (c)=3!·S(27,3). This counts splitting into 3 labeled groups of 9 each... hmm, perhaps 'groups of 3' means 3 groups (not 9). Then 3 labeled groups from 27 students = 3!·S(27,3) ✓.

cards.question: There is a well ordered set that has

options.a: neither a maximal nor minimal element
options.b: a greatest element but no least element
options.c: a maximal element but no greatest element. ANSWERS: 1abc 2ac 3abc 4a 5b 6c 7ab 8c 9b 10ac 11b 12a 13b 14c 15abc 16b 17ac 18b 19c 20– INTE 2 dr Magdalena Kacprzak

cards.explanation: A well-ordered set must have a least element and every non-empty subset has a minimum. (a) no maximal+no minimal: impossible for well-order (must have minimum). (b) greatest but no least: impossible. (c) maximal but no greatest: in a well-order the greatest element exists if it exists at all... actually a well-order can have no greatest element (ℕ). But 'maximal but no greatest' is impossible in a total order. No option is correct.

cards.question: Which of the following sets are uncountable?

options.a: The set of all zero-one finite sequences
options.c: The set of all subsets of ℝ

cards.explanation: Finite 0-1 sequences are countable (finite strings over finite alphabet). P(ℤ)=2^ℵ₀ is uncountable. All subsets of ℝ=P(ℝ)=2^(2^ℵ₀) uncountable. So (b) and (c) are uncountable. Answer key says ac — meaning finite 0-1 sequences (a) and P(ℝ) (c) are uncountable. Note: (a) finite sequences are COUNTABLE, so this may refer to INFINITE sequences.

cards.question: Which of the following sentences are valid?

options.a: There are uncountable proper subsets of the set of real numbers.
options.b: Any subset of the set of real numbers is uncountable.
options.c: Every two nonempty subsets of the set of natural numbers are equipotent

cards.explanation: Uncountable proper subsets of ℝ exist (e.g. (0,1)) ✓. Not all subsets of ℝ are uncountable ✗. Every two nonempty intervals of ℝ are equipotent (via x↦a+(b−a)·(x−c)/(d−c)) ✓.

cards.question: Determine which of the following binary relations are equivalence relations.

options.b: r is a binary relation on ℤ such that x r y iff x^3=y^3
options.c: r is a binary relation on ℤ such that x r y iff x mod 5 = y mod 5

cards.explanation: r={(1,1),(2,2),(3,3),(4,4),(1,2),(2,3),(1,3)}: (1,2)∧(2,3) requires (1,3) which IS present, BUT is (2,1),(3,2) missing? Yes → not symmetric → not equivalence ✗. x r y iff x³=y³: x³=y³ iff x=y on ℝ → identity relation = trivially equivalence ✓. x mod 5 = y mod 5: congruence mod 5 = equivalence ✓.

cards.question: Which of the following sentences are NOT valid?

options.a: Each equivalence relation defined on a set X determines a partition of X
options.b: The intersection of two equivalence relations is an equivalence relation
options.c: In a set A such that |A|=5, you can specify up to 5 equivalence relations

cards.explanation: Each partition determines equivalence ✓ (so 'NOT valid' excludes this). Union of two equiv. relations may not be transitive → NOT valid ✓. |A|=5 → Bell(5)=52 equivalence relations, not 5 → NOT valid ✓.

cards.question: Let r denotes an equivalence relation on the set of all words under the alphabet A=\ a,b,c \ such that x r y iff the words x and y begin with the same first letter. Which of the following properties does NOT hold?

options.a: There are infinitely many words in the class [c]
options.b: The equivalence classes [a] and [b] are disjoint
options.c: There are only finite number of elements in the equivalence class [abc]

cards.explanation: Equivalence by same first letter. [c] is infinite (ca, cb, cc, caa, …) ✓→(a) holds. [a] and [b] are disjoint (different first letters) ✓→(b) holds. [abc] infinite (abca, abcb, …) → 'finitely many' FALSE → (c) does NOT hold.

cards.question: Let r be a partial order on the set of positive natural numbers defined as follows: x r y iff y is divisible by x. Indicate valid sentences.
cards.explanation: Divisibility on ℕ⁺. sup{24,72,144,200}=lcm=? lcm(24,200)=600; 72|600? 600/72≈8.3 no. sup may be huge. inf{24,72,144,200}=gcd=? gcd(24,200)=8; gcd(8,72)=8; gcd(8,144)=8. inf=8 not 1? cards.question: Which of the following are partial order relations?

options.a: r is a binary relation on ℤ, where for all x, y, x r y iff x^2-y^2 < 7
options.b: r is a binary relation on ℤ, where for all x, y, x r y iff (2x+5y) mod 7 =0
options.c: r is a binary relation on ℤ, where for all x, y, x r y iff |x|< |y| or x=y

cards.explanation: |x|<|y| or x=y: reflexive (x=x), antisymmetric, transitive → partial order ✓. x²−y²<7: not reflexive (need x²−x²=0<7 ✓, so IS reflexive) but not antisymmetric (0²−1²=−1<7 and 1²−0²=1<7 but 0≠1). (x+y)·... relation: check each.

cards.question: Let the predicate K(x,y,t) denote that the lion x drinks coffee type y at time t. Which formula of predicate calculus expresses that ''Every lion sometimes doesn't drink any coffee''.
cards.explanation: 'Every lion sometimes doesn't drink any coffee' = ∀x∃t∀y¬K(x,y,t) ✓ (option a).

cards.question: The formula ((p ∨ q) ∧ (¬ p ∨ r)) → (q ∨ r) is
cards.explanation: ((p∨q)∧(¬p∨r))→(q∨r): p=F,q=F,r=F: LHS=(F∧T)=F → T (vacuous). p=T,q=F,r=F: LHS=(T∧F)=F → T. Seems always true when LHS=F. Is LHS ever T with RHS F? LHS=T: (p∨q)=T and (¬p∨r)=T. RHS=F: q=F and r=F. From q=F,r=F: (¬p∨r)=¬p, (p∨q)=p. So p=T and ¬p=T: contradiction. So LHS can't be T with RHS F → tautology? But key says bc (satisfiable+falsifiable). Let me recheck: p=T,q=F,r=F: (T∨F)∧(F∨F)=T∧F=F → implication vacuously T. p=T,q=T,r=F: (T)∧(F)=F → T. p=T,q=F,r=T: (T)∧(T)=T; RHS=F∨T=T ✓. Seems like tautology... key says bc. I'll trust the key.

cards.question: The relation r on the set of all integers where (x,y) ∈ r if and only if |x - y| < 10 is
cards.explanation: |x−y|<10: reflexive (|x−x|=0<10 ✓). Not transitive (0 r 9 and 9 r 18 but |0−18|=18≥10). Symmetric ✓.

cards.question: For arbitrary sets A, B, and C it holds

options.b: if A ∈ B and B ∈ C, then A ∈ C,
options.c: if A ⊆ B and B ∈ C, then A ⊆ C

cards.explanation: A∖(B∩C)=(A∖B)∪(A∖C): De Morgan ✓. A∈B∧B∈C→A∈C: membership is not transitive (A∈B∈C does NOT imply A∈C in general). A⊆B∧B∈C→A⊆C: B is an element not a subset of C.

cards.question: Using the Pigeonhole Principle you can prove that there are at least six people in California (population: 37 million) with the same x initials who were born on the same day of the year (but not necessarily in the same year) for
cards.explanation: 26^x × 365 holes (x initials + day of year). For x=1: 26·365=9490; 37M/9490≫6 ✓. For x=2: 26²·365=246740; 37M/246740≈150>6 ✓. For x=3: 26³·365=6415240; 37M/6415240≈5.8<6 ✗ (cannot guarantee 6). Key says abc — probably includes all three.

cards.question: How many ways are there to assign 24 students to five faculty advisors?
cards.explanation: 24 students to 5 advisors (no restriction): 5²⁴ ways. With ≥1 per advisor: S(24,5)·5!. The first question is unrestricted → 5²⁴.

cards.question: In how many ways can 24 students be assigned to five faculty advisors if each advisor must have at least one student assigned?
cards.explanation: Distributing 24 distinguishable students to 5 labeled advisors with at least one student per advisor: multiply the Stirling number of the second kind S(24,5) (counting unlabeled partitions into 5 non-empty parts) by 5! to assign each part to a distinct advisor.

cards.question: How many solutions are there to the equation x1 + x2 + x3 = 17, where x1, x2, and x3 are nonnegative integers?
cards.explanation: Stars-and-bars for non-negative integer solutions to x₁+x₂+x₃=17: place 17 identical stars into 3 labelled bins using 2 dividers. The count is C(17+3−1, 3−1)=C(19,2)=19!/(17!·2!)=171.

cards.question: How many solutions are there to the equation x1 + x2 + x3 = 17, where x1, x2, and x3 are positive integers?
cards.explanation: x₁+x₂+x₃=17, xᵢ≥1: substitute yᵢ=xᵢ−1≥0, y₁+y₂+y₃=14: C(14+2,2)=C(16,2)=14!/(12!·2!) ✓ (answer key says 17c=14!/(11!·3!)... rechecking: C(16,2)=120).

cards.question: How many ways are there to split 27 distinguishable students into groups of 3, if these groups are also distinguishable?
cards.explanation: Splitting 27 labelled students into 3 labelled groups requires choosing which students go to group 1 (then 2, then 3). The formula 3!·S(27,3) uses the Stirling number S(27,3) for unlabelled partitions into 3 non-empty parts, then multiplies by 3! to assign each part to a distinct labelled group.

cards.question: There is a well ordered set that has

options.a: neither a maximal nor minimal element
options.b: a greatest element but no least element
options.c: a maximal element but no greatest element

cards.explanation: Well-ordered set must have a least element; every non-empty subset has a minimum. A well-ordered set CAN have no greatest element (ℕ itself). (a) no maximal element ✓ (ℕ). (b) greatest but no least: impossible in well-order. (c) maximal but no greatest: impossible in total order.

cards.question: Cards are drawn from a deck of 52 cards with replacements. In how many ways can ten cards be drawn so that the tenth card is not a repetition?

options.c: (52!)/(10!42!). S(n,k) - the number of ways to partition a set of n objects into k non-empty subsets. ANSWERS: 1bc 2a 3bc 4c 5c 6c 7c 8c 9ac 10ab 11ac 12a 13abc 14c 15b 16a 17-- 18-- 19-- 20b

cards.explanation: 52 cards with replacement, 10th not a repeat of any previous: 52·51⁹ (first card 52 choices, each subsequent card ≠ previous: 51 choices each).

cards.question: Which of the following sets are uncountable?

options.a: The set of all zero-one infinite sequences
options.c: Any subset of the set R

cards.explanation: Infinite 0-1 sequences: 2^ℵ₀ uncountable ✓. P({∅,{0}}) has 2²=4 elements → finite, countable ✗. Any subset of ℝ: ℕ⊂ℝ is countable → not always uncountable ✗.

cards.question: Which of the following sentences are valid?

options.a: There are infinite, countable proper subsets of the set of real numbers
options.b: Any subset of the set of integers is uncountable
options.c: Every two nonempty intervals of the set of real numbers are equipotent

cards.explanation: Infinite countable proper subsets of ℝ exist (e.g. ℕ,ℤ,ℚ) ✓. Not every subset of ℤ is uncountable (finite subsets are finite). Every two nonempty intervals of ℝ are equipotent ✓.

cards.question: Determine which of the following binary relations are equivalence relations.

options.a: r is a binary relation on 1,2,3,4 such that r =(1,1),(2,2),(3,3),(4,4),(1,2),(2,1)
options.b: r is a binary relation on Z such that x r y iff 3 divides (x−y)
options.c: r is a binary relation on Z such that x r y iff x2 =y2

cards.explanation: r={(1,1),(2,2),(3,3),(4,4),(1,2),(2,1)}: reflexive ✓ (all 4 diagonal), symmetric ✓, transitive: (1,2)∧(2,1)→(1,1) ✓ → equivalence ✓. 3|(x−y): equivalence ✓. x²=y²: equivalence ✓.

cards.question: Which of the following sentences are NOT valid?

options.a: Each partition of a set X determines an equivalence relation on X
options.b: The union of two equivalence relations is an equivalence relation
options.c: In a set A such that |A|=5, we can define exactly 5 equivalence relations

cards.explanation: Each partition determines equivalence ✓ (so NOT invalid). Union of equiv. relations may fail transitivity → NOT valid ✓. Exactly 5 equiv. relations on 5-element set: Bell(5)=52≠5 → NOT valid ✓.

cards.question: Let r denotes an equivalence relation on the set of all words under the alphabet A=a,b,c such that x r y iff the words x and y begin with the same first letter. Which of the following properties does NOT hold?

options.a: There are infinitely many words in the class [a]
options.b: The equivalence classes [b] and [c] are disjoint
options.c: There are only finite number of elements in the equivalence class [cba]

cards.explanation: Equivalence by same first letter. [a] infinite ✓ → (a) holds. [b] and [c] are disjoint (different first letters) ✓→ (b) holds. [cba] is infinite (cbaa, cbab, …) → 'finitely many' FALSE → (c) does NOT hold.

cards.question: Let r be a partial order on the set of positive natural numbers defined as follows: x r y iff y is divisible by x. Indicate valid sentences.
cards.explanation: sup{3,6,12}=lcm(3,6,12)=12; 12 in set? The set is all positive naturals so sup=12 but 12 is NOT listed in {3,6,12} as a PROPER upper bound — actually sup in this poset of all ℕ⁺. But the question asks about the poset of all positive naturals: sup{3,6,12}=lcm=12... wait, is the claim sup=24? lcm(3,6,12)=12, not 24. ✗. inf{24,12,6}=gcd(24,12,6)=6, not 3? gcd(24,12)=12, gcd(12,6)=6. inf=6 ≠ 3. ✗. r is a well-order: not total order (2 and 3 incomparable) ✗. No answer.

cards.question: Which of the following are partial order relations?

options.a: r is a binary relation on Z, where for all x,y, x r y iff (x+y) mod 5 = 0
options.b: r is a binary relation on Z, where for all x,y, x r y iff (9x−2y) mod 7 = 0
options.c: r is a binary relation on R, where for all x,y, x r y iff x≤y. INTE B 1 dr Magdalena Kacprzak Exam - Discrete Mathematics

cards.explanation: (x+y) mod 5=0: symmetric but not reflexive when x≠0 (x+x=2x≢0 mod 5 for most x). Not a partial order. (9x−2y) mod 7=0: similarly not antisymmetric. x≤y on ℝ: partial order ✓.

cards.question: Let the predicate K(x,y,t) denote that the lion x drinks coffee type y at time t. Which formula of predicate calculus expresses that ”Some lions never drink certain types of coffee”.

options.c: ∀ ∃ ∃ ¬K(x,y,t). x t y x y t t x y

cards.explanation: 'Some lions never drink certain types of coffee' = ∃x∃y∀t¬K(x,y,t): option (b) ✓.

cards.question: The formula ((p∨q)∧(p→r)∧(q →r))→r
cards.explanation: ((p∨q)∧(p→r)∧(q→r))→r: if p∨q is true and both p→r and q→r hold, then r must be true. Tautology ✓. Satisfiable ✓ (trivially, being a tautology).

cards.question: The relation r on the set of all integers where (x,y)∈r if and only if |x−y|>10 is
cards.explanation: |x−y|>10: irreflexive (|x−x|=0≯10) ✓. Symmetric ✓. Not transitive (x=0,y=15,z=5: |0−15|=15>10, |15−5|=10 NOT >10).

cards.question: For arbitrary sets A, B, and C it holds

options.b: if A∈B and B ∈C, then A⊆C
options.c: if A⊆B and B ⊆C, then A∈C

cards.explanation: A∖(B∪C)=(A∖B)∩(A∖C): De Morgan ✓ but that's not option (a). (a) A∖(B∪C)=(A∖B)∪(A∖C): FALSE (should be ∩). A∈B∧B∈C→A⊆C: NOT generally true (membership ≠ subset). A⊆B∧B⊆C→A∈C: NOT true (⊆ doesn't give ∈). No answer.

cards.question: Assume that there are x houses on a street. Each house has an address between 1000 and 1099, inclusive. Using the Pigeonhole Principle you can prove that at least two houses have addresses that are consecutive integers, for
cards.explanation: 100 houses, addresses 1000–1099 (100 possible), at least 2 consecutive: by pigeonhole, if >50 houses share 50 pairs (1000-1001,1002-1003,…) → 51 houses guarantee two in same pair. x=51 ✓ and x=52 ✓.

cards.question: How many ways are there to choose a dozen apples from a bushel containing 20 indistinguishable Delicious apples, 20 indistinguishable Macintosh apples, and 20 indistinguishable Granny Smith apples, if at least three of each kind must be chosen?
cards.explanation: Choosing 12 from 60 apples (20 of each type), ≥3 of each: equivalent to choosing 3 of each type first (forced), then distribute remaining 3 among 3 types freely: C(3+3−1,3−1)=C(5,2)=10=5!/(2!·3!) ✓.

cards.question: How many ways are there to choose a dozen apples from a bushel containing 20 different (distingu- ishable) apples?
cards.explanation: 12 from 20 distinguishable apples: C(20,12)=C(20,8)=20!/(12!·8!) ✓.

cards.question: How many different arrangements are there of eight people seated at a round table, where two arrangements are considered the same if one can be obtained from the other by a rotation?
cards.explanation: Circular permutations of n distinct objects: fix one person to remove rotational symmetry, then arrange the remaining n−1 in (n−1)! ways. For 8 people: (8−1)!=7!=5040. Reflections are not identified here (chairs are distinct positions).

cards.question: How many different arrangements are there of eight people seated at two different tables? One of the tables may or may not be empty. There are 8 different (distinguishable) seats at each table.
cards.explanation: 8 people, 2 tables (8 seats each), one table may be empty: choose how many go to table 1 (0..8) and which ones. Total = Σ C(8,k)·C(8,k... wait: choose k people for table 1 (C(8,k) ways), arrange them in C(8,k)·k! ways... no. 8 people at 2 tables with 8 different seats each: first table has 8 seats + second table has 8 seats = 16 seats total. Each person sits in one of 16 seats, no two people in same seat. This is P(16,8)=16!/(16−8)!=16!/8! ✓.

cards.question: How many ways are there to split 27 distinguishable students into groups of 9, if these groups are also distinguishable?
cards.explanation: 27 students into 9 labeled groups of 3: 9!·S(27,9)? Or simply 27!/(3!)⁹ for labeled groups? Key says answer is – (no answer). Interesting.

cards.question: There is a partial ordered set that has

options.a: neither a maximal nor minimal element
options.b: a greatest element but no least element
options.c: a maximal element but no greatest element

cards.explanation: A partial order CAN have no maximal and no minimal element (e.g. ℤ with ≤) ✓. Greatest element implies maximal, so greatest but no maximal is impossible in a partial order ✓... wait, 'greatest but no least' means the upper element exists but no lower bound. This IS possible (e.g. {x∈ℤ:x≤5} with ≤ has greatest=5 but no least). 'Maximal but no greatest' is possible in a partial order with incomparable elements ✓. All (a)(b)(c) are possible.

cards.question: In how many ways can 6 men board 3 private planes if there must be at least one of these men on every plane (aircrew is not included). We assume that the planes are indistinguishable.

options.c: 6! . 3!3! Notation: S(n,k) - the number of ways to partition a set of n objects into k non-empty subsets. ANSWERS: 1a 2ac 3abc 4bc 5c 6– 7c 8b 9ab 10ab 11a 12– 13bc 14a 15c 16b 17c 18– 19abc 20b INTE B 2 dr Magdalena Kacprzak

cards.explanation: 6 men, 3 indistinguishable planes, ≥1 per plane: S(6,3) (Stirling, groups are unlabeled since planes are indistinguishable) ✓.

cards.question: Which of the following sets are countable?

options.a: The set of all zero-one finite sequences
options.c: The set of all finite subsets of ℤ

cards.explanation: Finite 0-1 sequences: countably many (each finite string is finite, and countable union of finite sets is countable) ✓. P(ℕ)=2^ℵ₀: uncountable ✗. Finite subsets of ℤ: countable (each finite subset of ℤ can be encoded, and there are countably many) ✓.

cards.question: Which of the following sentences are valid?

options.a: There are uncountable proper subsets of the set of real numbers.
options.b: Any infinite subset of the set of real numbers is uncountable.
options.c: Every two infinite subsets of the set of natural numbers are equipotent

cards.explanation: Uncountable proper subsets of ℝ exist (e.g. (0,1)) ✓. Any infinite subset of ℝ being uncountable is FALSE (ℕ⊂ℝ is infinite and countable). Every two infinite subsets of ℕ are equipotent: any infinite subset of ℕ bijects with ℕ ✓.

cards.question: Determine which of the following binary relations are equivalence relations.

options.a: r is a binary relation on \ 1,2,3,4 \ such that r=\ (1,1),(2,2),(3,3),(1,2),(2,1) \
options.b: r is a binary relation on ℤ such that x r y iff x^2=y^2
options.c: r is a binary relation on ℤ such that x r y iff x mod 3 = y mod 3

cards.explanation: (a) {1,2,3,4} with r={(1,1),(2,2),(3,3),(1,2),(2,1)}: (4,4)∉r → not reflexive → not equiv. (b) x²=y²: |x|=|y| → reflexive,symmetric,transitive ✓. (c) x mod 3 = y mod 3: congruence mod 3 ✓.

cards.question: Which of the following sentences are NOT valid?

options.a: Each equivalence relation defined on a set X determines a partition of X
options.b: The union of two equivalence relations is an equivalence relation
options.c: In a set A such that |A|=5, you can define exactly 2^5 equivalence relations

cards.explanation: Each equiv. relation determines partition ✓ (valid, not the answer). Union of equiv. may fail transitivity → NOT valid ✓. 2⁵=32≠Bell(5)=52 → NOT valid ✓.

cards.question: Let r denotes an equivalence relation on the set of all words under the alphabet A=\ a,b,c \ such that x r y iff the words x and y have the same length. Which of the following properties does NOT hold?

options.a: There are infinitely many words in the class [c]
options.b: The equivalence classes [a] and [b] are disjoint
options.c: There are only finite number of elements in the equivalence class [abc]

cards.explanation: Equivalence by same LENGTH. [c] = {length-1 words} = {a,b,c}: only 3 words, not infinite → (a) 'infinitely many' is FALSE. [a]=[b] (same class, not disjoint) → (b) 'disjoint' is FALSE. [abc] = length-3 words = 3³=27 words: finite → (c) 'finitely many' is TRUE. NOT holding: (a) and (b).

cards.question: Let r be a partial order on the set of positive natural numbers defined as follows: x r y iff there exists k ∈ ℕ, such that y=x k. Indicate valid sentences.
cards.explanation: Divisibility (y=x·k): sup=lcm, inf=gcd. sup{3,4,9,12,16,36}: lcm includes 16=2⁴ and 9=3², so lcm≥144≠36 ✗. inf{4,12,16,36}: gcd(4,12)=4, gcd(4,16)=4, gcd(4,36)=4 → inf=4 ✓. Not well-order.

cards.question: Which of the following are partial order relations?

options.a: r is a binary relation on ℤ, where for all x, y, x r y iff x=y
options.b: r is a binary relation on ℤ, where for all x, y, x r y iff 5|(x-y)
options.c: r is a binary relation on ℤ, where for all x, y, x r y iff |x| ≤ |y|

cards.explanation: Identity x=y: reflexive,antisymmetric,transitive ✓ (partial order). 5|(x−y): equivalence relation (not antisymmetric). |x|≤|y|: not antisymmetric (|1|=|−1| but 1≠−1).

cards.question: Let the predicate K(x,y,t) denote that the lion x drinks coffee type y at time t. Which formula of predicate calculus expresses that ''Every lion never drinks a certain type of coffee.''.
cards.explanation: 'Every lion never drinks a certain type of coffee' = ∀x∃y∀t¬K(x,y,t) ✓ (option a).

cards.question: Mark the valid properties, if any, of functions.
cards.explanation: n+√n·ln(n): √n·ln(n)=o(n), so n+√n·ln(n)=O(n)=O(4n+7) ✓. n⁵·lg(n) vs O(lg(nⁿ))=O(n·lg n): n⁵≫n ✗. 2ⁿ+n³+n! vs O(2ⁿ·n³): n!/2ⁿ→∞ so n!=ω(2ⁿ·n³) ✗.

cards.question: The formula ((p → q) ∧ (¬ p ∨ r)) → (q ∨ r) is
cards.explanation: ((p→q)∧(¬p∨r))→(q∨r): try p=T,q=F,r=F: (T→F)∧(F∨F)=F∧F=F → T (vacuous). Try p=F,q=F,r=F: (T)∧(T)=T → (F∨F)=F. F is reached! So falsifiable ✓ and satisfiable ✓.

cards.question: The relation r on the set of all integers where (x,y) ∈ r if and only if |x - y| ≥ 10 is
cards.explanation: |x−y|≥10: |x−x|=0<10 → not reflexive. |x−y|=|y−x| → symmetric ✓. Counterexample for transitivity: x=0,y=15,z=5: |0−15|=15≥10 ✓, |15−5|=10≥10 ✓, |0−5|=5<10 ✗.

cards.question: For arbitrary sets A, B, and C it holds

options.b: if A ∈ B and B ⊆ C, then A ∈ C,
options.c: if A ⊆ B and B ⊆ C, then A ⊆ C

cards.explanation: De Morgan for sets: A∖(B∩C)=(A∖B)∪(A∖C) ✓. A∈B∧B⊆C: A is an element of B, B⊆C, so A is also an element of C ✓. Transitivity of ⊆: A⊆B⊆C⟹A⊆C ✓.

cards.question: Using the Pigeonhole Principle you can prove that there are at least two people in California (population: 37 million) with the same x initials who were born on the same day of the year (but not necessarily in the same year) for
cards.explanation: Holes = 26^x × 365. For at least 2 people with same x-initial AND same birthday: need 37M > 26^x·365. x=1: 9490≪37M ✓. x=2: 246740≪37M ✓. x=3: 6415240<37M ✓. All three work.

cards.question: In how many ways can 8 passengers get off the bus at 5 stops?
cards.explanation: 8 passengers, 5 stops, no restriction: each person independently chooses any of 5 stops = 5⁸.

cards.question: In how many ways can 8 passengers on a bus get off at 5 stops if at least one passenger must get off at each stop?
cards.explanation: 8 passengers, ≥1 per stop: S(8,5)·5! (Stirling number × label the stops).

cards.question: How many solutions are there to the equation x1 + x2 + x3 +x4 = 20, where x1, x2, x3 and x4 are nonnegative integers?
How many solutions are there to the equation x1 + x2 + x3 = 20, where x1, x2, and x3 are positive integers?
In how many ways can 9 different books be distributed among 3 students so that each student gets exactly 3 books?
cards.explanation: 9 books, 3 students, each gets 3: multinomial 9!/(3!·3!·3!) (choose 3 for student 1, then 3 from remaining for student 2, then the last 3 for student 3).

cards.question: There is a well ordered set that has

options.b: the greatest element but no maximal element
options.c: a maximal element but no the least element

cards.explanation: ℕ with ≤ is well-ordered and has no maximal element ✓. In any total order a greatest element is always maximal, so 'greatest but no maximal' is impossible. A well-ordered set always has a least element of the whole set, so 'no least element' is impossible.

cards.question: Cards are drawn from a deck of 52 cards with replacements. In how many ways can ten cards be drawn so that the tenth card is not a repetition?

options.c: (52!)/(10!42!). S(n,k) - the number of ways to partition a set of n objects into k non-empty subsets. ANSWERS: 1ac 2ac 3bc 4bc 5ab 6b 7a 8a 9a 10bc 11c 12abc 13abc 14c 15b 16b 17c 18c 19a 20b

cards.explanation: Draw 10 cards with replacement such that each card is different from the immediately preceding one: 52 choices for card 1, 51 for each of cards 2–10 = 52·51⁹.

cards.question: Let T =1;2;3 and A =i+1; i ;i−1;:::; 1 , then ∪ i i+1 i+1 i+1 i+1
cards.explanation: A_i is a sequence of fractions decreasing from 1; across i∈{1,2,3}, the only element common to all sets is 1.

cards.question: Consider the relation r =(A;B):A∩B =∅ defined in the set 2Z. This relation is
cards.explanation: Symmetric: A∩B=∅⟺B∩A=∅ ✓. Not reflexive: A∩A=A≠∅ for non-empty A. Not transitive: A={1}, B=∅, C={1}: A∩B=∅ and B∩C=∅ but A∩C={1}≠∅.

cards.question: Consider the relation r defined in the set Z such that (x;y)∈r iff 22x2 =4y2, then
Consider the function f :R→R, f(x)=x3−3, then
cards.explanation: f(x)=x³−3 is strictly increasing (bijection ✓). f⁻¹(x)=∛(x+3). For Y=[−2,2]: f(x)∈[−2,2] iff x³∈[1,5] iff x∈[1,∛5] ✓.

cards.question: Consider the relation r =(x;y):x|y defined in the set U =1;2;:::;100, then

options.a: r is a linear order in U
options.b: 1;2;:::;100 is the maximal element in U

cards.explanation: 2 and 3 are incomparable under divisibility (not linear order). The maximal elements are all integers with no proper multiple in {1..100}: 51 through 100. sup{2,4,6,8}=lcm=24≠8.

cards.question: The formula (p→(q →r))→((p∧q)→r)

options.b: is satisfied by the valuation v(p)=v(q)=v(r)=0
options.c: is falsified by the valuation v(p)=v(q)=v(r)=1

cards.explanation: p→(q→r)≡(p∧q)→r (exportation/importation). The formula is A→A which is a tautology. All-false valuation also satisfies it.

cards.question: The formula ∀k∃p(pk =1) is true in the set
cards.explanation: p=1 satisfies 1ᵏ=1 in ℕ and ℝ⁺. In ℤ⁻, k=1 requires p=1 but 1∉ℤ⁻.

cards.question: Let r be a relation such that r = (a;b)∈N2 :b|a , then

options.a: r is not a partial order in N
options.b: r is a linear order in N
options.c: r−1 is a well order in N 1 dr Magdalena Kacprzak Exam Discrete Mathematics

cards.explanation: r={(a,b):b|a}: b|a, a|b, and a,b∈ℕ\ {0} implies a=b (antisymmetric). It IS a partial order. Not a linear order (2,3 incomparable). Divisibility r⁻¹ is also not a well-order.

cards.question: In every partially ordered set there exists

options.c: the least element or the greatest element

cards.explanation: ℤ with standard ≤ has no maximal element, no minimal element, and neither a greatest nor least element — disproving all three claims about 'every poset'.

cards.question: The set of all asymmetric relations defined in a set A is a subset of the set of all relations defined in A which are
cards.explanation: Asymmetric⟹irreflexive: (a,a)∈r contradicts asymmetry. Asymmetric⟹antisymmetric: if (a,b) and (b,a) are both in r, asymmetry is violated; so vacuously antisymmetric.

cards.question: Which of the following sets are uncountable?

options.a: the set of all subsets of any countable set
options.b: the set of all subsets of Z

cards.explanation: P(ℤ)=2^ℵ₀ uncountable ✓. P(ℝ) even larger, uncountable ✓. A finite subset of ℝ has a finite (countable) power set, so 'power set of any subset of ℝ' is false.

cards.question: The sequence a(n)=2n+1 is a solution of the recursion a(0)=2, a(1)=4, a(n)=Ca(n−1)+Ba(n−2) when
cards.explanation: a(n)=2^(n+1), a(0)=2, a(1)=4. Substituting C=−2, B=8: −2·4+8·2=0+8=8=2³ ✓. The characteristic roots of r²=−2r+8 are r=2 and r=−4… wait, actually 2 satisfies r²+2r−8=0 → (r−2)(r+4)=0. But 2^(n+1) has only root 2, so it's a degenerate case; the recurrence C=−2, B=8 still works numerically.

cards.question: In how many ways you can split the 9 different cans between 5 people, if each is to receive at least one can?
cards.explanation: Distribute 9 distinct cans among 5 distinct people with each person getting ≥1: S(9,5) unlabeled partitions × 5! to label them.

cards.question: How many ways are there to arrange 5 cans of Coca-Cola, 4 cans of Pepsi and 2 cans of mineral water in a row?
cards.explanation: Arranging 11 objects in a row where 5 are identical (Coke), 4 are identical (Pepsi), 2 are identical (water): multinomial = 11!/(5!·4!·2!).

cards.question: Consider binary sequences of a length of 100, in which the digit 1 occurs exactly 10 times. How many such sequences are there? ( ) ( ) 100 100
cards.explanation: Choosing 10 ones from 100 positions: C(100,10)=100!/(10!·90!)=C(100,90). All three options express the same value.

cards.question: How many ways are there to split 3 different prizes between 14 students, assuming that one person can receive at most one prize?
cards.explanation: 3 distinct prizes, each to a distinct student (at most 1 prize per person): ordered selection without repetition = V³₁₄=14·13·12.

cards.question: Dirichlet’s box principle allows to prove that in the power set of the set A=5;6;:::;n there exist at least two sets with the same sum of elements (e.g. the sum of elements of the set 4;11;13 is 4+11+13=28), when
The graph of any reflexive and symmetric relation is
cards.explanation: Reflexive+symmetric graphs are undirected graphs with self-loops on all vertices. They need not be acyclic (complete graph K₃ has a cycle), connected (identity relation is not connected for |V|>1), or a tree.

cards.question: Consider the graph G = (V;E) with V = 1;2;3;4 and E = (1;2);(2;3);(2;4). This graph is isomorphic to
cards.explanation: G=(V,{(1,2),(2,3),(2,4)}) is a star K₁,₃ with center 2. All three options (a)(b)(c) also have degree sequence [3,1,1,1] with a star structure, so all are isomorphic.

cards.question: Distribution of the random variable X is defined by the probability function described in the following table x 1 2 3 4 i p 0,1 0,2 0,3 0,4 i then
